title: Darwin's Paradox!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently frame Darwin's Paradox! as a single-player, story-driven 3D platformer-adventure with puzzle solving and light stealth. The strongest recurring gameplay signals are 'platforming sequences,' 'moments of puzzle-solving,' and verbs like swim, wall-climb, hide, infiltrate, jump, camouflage, and shoot ink to overcome traps and escape. The presentation is described as a cinematic animated-movie-like adventure in a mysterious industrial complex with sprawling handcrafted levels, which supports a linear/level-based campaign structure and a secondary cinematic action-adventure flavor. Combat is not presented as dominant; instead, stealthy avoidance, traversal, and puzzle progression appear to define the core loop, making action_platformer the best fit.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: most candidates capture the anchor's authored platform-adventure structure, puzzle-forward progression, and stealthy escape through dangerous environments. The main gap is perspective/gamefeel spread—some picks are 2D cinematic puzzle-platformers while others are broader 3D exploration adventures—so the set mixes very close structure comps with looser animal-protagonist thematic neighbors.
anchor_summary: Darwin's Paradox! reads as a single-player cinematic action-platformer/adventure built around traversal, trap avoidance, light stealth, and environmental puzzle-solving in an authored campaign. Its identity is less combat-driven than movement- and escape-driven, with bespoke creature abilities used to infiltrate and survive a hostile industrial space.

title: Little Nemo and the Guardians of Slumberland

They describe classic side-scrolling platformer play, ability-gated progression via toys and pajamas, exploration of interconnected domains, boss fights, collectibles, and unlocking new abilities to access new pathways. That combination strongly supports metroidvania over a simpler action-platformer label.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong: most candidates are colorful, approachable 2D exploration platformers with ability unlocks and boss progression. Best fits are other light-to-midweight metroidvanias with strong traversal gating. title: Last Man Sitting

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a roguelite action shooter built around surviving progressively harder waves, leveling up during runs, drafting power-ups, unlocking chair upgrades, and finding build synergies. The core loop is presented as single-player or up to 4-player co-op, with PvP arena/king-of-the-hill described as an extra mode rather than the main identity. Combat is dominant, fast, and hybridized between shooting and hacking/slashing, with minibosses and bosses closing out wave progression. This strongly fits co_op_action_roguelite over arena_fps because the text emphasizes roguelite run structure, power-up drafting, co-op survival, and meta-upgrades rather than primarily match-based FPS competition.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around co-op roguelite shooters with build-focused runs and horde pressure. Its biggest gap is that only one candidate strongly reflects the anchor’s 4-player co-op, run-based horde survival from a third-person/arena-feeling angle; the rest skew more corridor/level-based FPS or single-player arcade action.
anchor_summary: Last Man Sitting is best read as a co-op action roguelite shooter: survive escalating enemy waves, level during runs, draft power-ups, build synergies, and use persistent upgrades/loadouts in solo or up-to-4-player co-op, with PvP as a side mode rather than the core identity.
gap_notes: No candidate closely matches the anchor's specific mix of wave-survival arena structure, 4-player co-op, and third-person shooter/melee hybrid combat.
The set leans heavily toward FPS roguelites, which fit progression and run structure but miss some of the anchor's broader arena-survival identity.
KILL KNIGHT captures intensity and swarm pressure, but its single-player isometric arcade structure makes it a much weaker recommendation than the co-op roguelite shooters.

title: Subliminal

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Subliminal as a first-person psychological horror experience centered on exploring handcrafted open-ended memory-spaces and solving light/perspective-based environmental puzzles. The Steam detailed description emphasizes hallucination-like instability, unsettling spaces, a pursuing inhuman figure, and psychologically terrifying encounters, which strongly supports psychological_horror over action_horror or survival_horror. Gameplay identity appears to be exploration plus puzzle-solving under threat rather than combat-driven play; chase/pressure sequences justify light survival-oriented encounter framing, but not dominant combat. Metacritic explicitly labels it 'First-Person Adventure,' while Steam copy repeatedly stresses psychological horror, environmental puzzle solving, and single-player progression through authored spaces.

overall_note: The rail has a solid core around first-person reality-warping puzzle play and psychological horror exploration, but it includes one clearly wrong title and misses a cleaner distinction between mechanical puzzle comps and horror-under-threat comps.
anchor_summary: Subliminal appears to be a single-player first-person psychological horror game built around exploration and environmental puzzle-solving, especially light and perspective manipulation, with intermittent chase/threat sequences rather than combat-heavy survival systems.
gap_notes: Superliminal and Viewfinder are the best mechanical matches because Subliminal's identity strongly emphasizes perspective/light-based spatial puzzles.
Layers of Fear and MADiSON fit the horror side well, but are less exact on puzzle structure and systemic environment manipulation.

title: Raccoin: Coin Pusher Roguelike

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam copy gives strong gameplay evidence: this is an arcade-style coin pusher built around repeated runs, score-chasing, and physics-driven coin drops, with roguelike deckbuilding layered on top through collectible special coins, 150 items, character-specific loadouts/playstyles, endless mode, and multiple difficulty levels. It is not literal pinball, but within the available taxonomy the closest fit is pinball because the core interaction is a non-combat arcade table/machine focused on chaining effects, manipulating a physical playfield, and maximizing payouts/scores. Secondary card_battler reflects the explicit 'roguelike deckbuilder' structure, though card play itself is absent, so it remains secondary rather than primary.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's unusual mix of arcade randomness/physics and roguelike buildcrafting reasonably well, but it leans a bit too heavily on broad 'dopamine roguelite' overlap. The strongest fits are other run-based games where a machine-like randomizer or physics board is the main interaction; more combat-centric or purely card-scoring comps are slightly looser.

title: Grime II

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and repeatedly identify GRIME II as an 'action-adventure metroidvania' and describe hallmark genre structure: side-scrolling exploration through a deep interconnected world, secret paths gated by newly acquired forms/abilities, platforming traversal, and combat used both for progression and discovery. title: Enter the chronosphere

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player, procedurally generated run-based top-down shooter/roguelike with a distinctive hybrid structure: turn-based tactical planning blended with real-time bullet-hell action, plus build synergies, unlockable characters, weapons, and missions. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a good-fit archetype for a single-player tactical/bullet-hell roguelike shooter of this kind. Available shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, tactical_shooter, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or extraction_shooter do not match the top-down run-based roguelike identity, and turn_based_tactics would also be misleading because the game is explicitly a shooter with hybrid real-time bullet-hell combat rather than primarily overhead squad tactics. Because the gameplay evidence is strong but does not map cleanly to an allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's hybrid tactical-shooter identity reasonably well, with especially strong matches around time-linked action, run-based combat, and top-down sci-fi roguelike structure. The main gap is that only a couple candidates really reflect the anchor's exact blend of bullet-hell shooter feel and move-to-advance tactical pacing; several others match the roguelike/top-down shooter side but lose the anchor's signature temporal combat model.
anchor_summary: Enter the Chronosphere is a single-player top-down roguelike shooter built around procedurally generated runs, ranged combat, exotic gear synergies, unlockable characters/progression, and a distinctive hybrid timing model where action advances when you move, creating turn-based tactical bullet-dodging inside a shooter structure.

title: Super Meat Boy 3D

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly define the game as a tough-as-nails 3D platformer centered on 'straight forward twitch reflex platforming' and 'precision platforming action.' The text emphasizes brutal but fair levels, repeated deaths from hazards, wall-leaping, and escalating difficulty, which strongly matches precision_platformer. Metacritic genre labeling supports third-person 3D platforming, while the copy's references to 'a bunch of levels,' Dark World levels, and boss fights support a level-based campaign structure with boss-centric encounters. Combat is not described as a primary loop, so the game fits precision platformer far better than action_platformer or 3d_collectathon; unlockable secrets are present, but collection is not framed as the core objective.

overall_note: The rail has a strong core around precision platforming, especially the original Super Meat Boy and Celeste. Its main weakness is perspective/structure drift: several candidates are 2D or mix in exploration/combat more heavily than the anchor’s straightforward 3D hazard-platforming identity.
anchor_summary: Super Meat Boy 3D is a single-player, level-based 3D precision platformer built around brutal-but-fair hazard gauntlets, rapid retries, mastery-heavy movement, and twitch-reflex execution, with boss fights and secrets as secondary elements rather than combat or collection as the core loop.
gap_notes: There is only one clear 3D precision-platforming comp here; the rest mostly compare on challenge model rather than perspective.

title: Hozy

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Hozy as a cozy restoration/decorating simulation focused on cleaning, painting, furnishing, and interacting with objects across nine restoreable locations, with no timers, scores, penalties, or combat. The evidence is strong for a non-combat cozy renovation sim loop, but the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype set does not provide a clear fit for this gameplay identity. It is not well-supported as life_sim, management_tycoon, hidden_object_puzzle, or any action/adventure/RPG/shooter/racing archetype. Because the gameplay is clear yet falls outside the available archetypes, this should be rejected rather than forced into a poor match.

title: MARVEL MaXimum Collection

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame MARVEL MaXimum Collection as a retro compilation/archive containing multiple distinct legacy Marvel games and versions across arcade, 8-bit, 16-bit, and portable platforms. The text explicitly says it is 'spanning multiple genres' and then lists materially different included games and modes: beat-'em-up action (X-Men: The Arcade Game), a platforming variant of Captain America and The Avengers, co-op side-scrolling action in Maximum Carnage/Separation Anxiety, and an infamously difficult shooter in Silver Surfer. Because the package aggregates several different gameplay identities rather than presenting one dominant, unified core loop, there is not a single taxonomy archetype that can be supported strongly enough. Mode labels like co-op, multiplayer, and single-player are also compilation-wide and do not resolve the gameplay identity problem.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid because most candidates match the anchor as preservation-minded retro compilations of legacy licensed games rather than as single-loop action games. The best fits are other multi-game classic collections with version comparisons and museum/QoL framing. The weakest fit is the genre-specific Capcom bundle, which overlaps on arcade co-op brawlers but misses the anchor's broader licensed-compilation identity.
anchor_summary: MARVEL MaXimum Collection is best understood as a retro compilation/archive rather than a single-game archetype: a licensed package of multiple 8/16-bit and arcade-era Marvel games spanning different genres, with preservation framing, version completeness, co-op support in some included titles, and modern quality-of-life features like rewind, save states, galleries, and online support for select games.
gap_notes: The strongest commonality here is 'retro compilation/archive with modern extras,' not one unified combat loop or genre.
Collections that preserve multiple legacy versions of licensed games fit better than single-genre action anthologies.
Candidates centered on documentary/preservation framing are especially relevant because the anchor emphasizes archive/history features as much as play.

title: Dying Light: The Beast Restored Land

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently identify this as a long-form open-world Dying Light campaign focused on exploration of a large handcrafted region, first-person melee combat against infected, grounded parkour traversal, scavenging/loot, day-night survival pressure, authored story progression, and optional shared-progression co-op. Restored Land is described as an enhanced version of the same core game with harsher survival rules, permanent world consequences, and one-life options, which intensify survival elements without changing the underlying gameplay identity. That supports open_world_action_adventure as the primary archetype, with strong action_horror and exploration_survival_adventure traits.

overall_note: The rail has one essential inclusion and a couple of solid franchise/genre neighbors, but it misses stronger direct Dying Light comps and includes one candidate whose gameplay structure is notably farther away.
anchor_summary: Enhanced package of Dying Light: The Beast: a first-person open-world zombie action-survival game built around parkour traversal, physical melee combat, scavenging/crafting, day-night pressure, story progression, and optional co-op, with Restored Land adding harsher survival rules and permanence rather than changing the core gameplay identity.
gap_notes: Dying Light: The Beast is the clear must-keep because Restored Land is an enhanced edition of that base game.
Dying Light is a very strong franchise benchmark thanks to the same first-person parkour-melee-day/night identity.
Dying Light 2 Stay Human: Bloody Ties fits through shared core mechanics, but as a DLC-focused slice with a more specialized tournament structure it is less ideal than the full base game would be.
Dead Island 2 is a reasonable adjacent zombie melee comp, but it lacks the anchor's parkour-led traversal identity.
State of Decay 2 is meaningfully more management/survival-sim oriented and third-person, making it a weak rail fit.

candidate_reviews.rationale: This is the base game for the anchor's enhanced edition, with effectively the same open-world first-person parkour, melee survival, progression, and co-op structure.
